title: Naive Bayes: Predicting with Probability
concept: Naive Bayes uses Bayes' theorem conceptually to predict a category based on evidence and historical probability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a key advantage of using k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) for recommendations in practice?

options.text: It is intuitive and makes recommendations based on similarity to items a user already likes or users with similar tastes
It operates instantly even with billions of items and users
It predicts continuous numeric values rather than categories

exercises.explain: Exactly! kNN is intuitive and works by finding the closest neighbors based on similarity, but it can slow down with massive datasets.

cards.title: Deciding what to wear
cards.body: If you see dark clouds, you might decide to grab a coat. You are looking at clues in your environment (evidence) and using your past experience to decide what is most likely to happen. In machine learning, we can use probability to do the exact same thing.

cards.title: What is Naive Bayes?
cards.body: Naive Bayes is a classification algorithm that uses probability and evidence to predict a category. Instead of measuring distance to neighbors, it calculates the mathematical likelihood of each category based on historical clues, and chooses the one with the highest probability.

cards.title: The detective's board
cards.body: Think of Naive Bayes as a detective trying to solve a case. Each clue has a probability of appearing if a suspect is guilty or innocent. The detective multiplies the probabilities of all the clues to see which scenario is more likely. The scenario with the highest probability wins!

cards.title: Will it rain today?
cards.body: A weather app wants to predict if it will rain based on historical data. If the app calculates an 80% probability of rain based on humidity and cloud cover, and only a 20% probability of dry weather, the model predicts "Rain" because it's the most likely category.

cards.title: Math, not gut feelings
cards.body: You might think using probability is like rolling dice or guessing. In reality, Naive Bayes uses precise mathematical calculations based on historical facts. It calculates exact percentages from actual occurrences in the training data to make its predictions.

left.text: Checks the physical closeness of neighboring points in the data.

right.text: Calculates the likelihood of each category based on historical clues.

exercises.prompt: Which algorithm makes predictions by calculating the mathematical probability of each category based on evidence?
exercises.explain: Yes! Naive Bayes classifies items by calculating the probability of each category and picking the most likely one.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Naive Bayes decides which category to predict by looking for the closest neighboring data points in a coordinate grid.
exercises.explain: False! Looking for nearby data points is the distance-based approach of kNN. Naive Bayes calculates probabilities from clues.

exercises.sentence: While kNN uses distance to classify data, Naive Bayes uses ___ to make its predictions.
exercises.explain: Naive Bayes calculates the mathematical probability of different categories based on the evidence it sees.

exercises.prompt: Which one of these represents a probability-based prediction made by a Naive Bayes model?

options.text: Email classification: 95% chance of Spam, 5% chance of Safe. Prediction: Spam.
Recommendation: This movie is recommended because 4 of your closest neighbors watched it.
Forecast: Tomorrow's temperature will be 74.5 degrees.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Naive Bayes evaluates categories using the probability of each class given the evidence.

exercises.prompt: You are building a system to sort customer support tickets into 'Billing', 'Tech Support', or 'Shipping'. You decide to use Naive Bayes. How will the model choose the category for a new ticket?

options.text: It will calculate the probability of each category based on the words in the ticket, and assign the ticket to the category with the highest probability.
It will find the single closest past ticket in the database and copy its category.
It will calculate the average word length of the ticket and match it to a numeric scale.
It will guess a category at random because it does not use historical data.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Naive Bayes looks at the evidence (words in the ticket) and calculates the probability for each category, then selects the category with the highest mathematical likelihood. You're ready to learn why we call this smart technique 'naive'!
